However, currently only Anthropic has completed the full cycle from "addition" to "deconstruction." 6. There's an even bigger shell beyond Harness. The five points of consensus discussed in Q1—Markdown as a state carrier, Git for transaction boundaries, the CLI renaissance (Agents need a structured text interface; a single `git diff --stat` command provides an overview of all changes), real-time testing (Agents modify a file and immediately run tests; test results become instant feedback signals for each step), and Skill for knowledge encapsulation—don't all belong to Harness. The larger framework is called Agentic Infrastructure, which consists of five layers: Context layer (what the agent can remember), Tool interface layer (what the agent can do), Harness layer (how the agent is managed), Knowledge layer (how the agent knows how to do things), and Economics layer (how much it costs to run the agent). Harness is the most crucial layer, but it's far from the whole story. Currently, the industry's attention is focused on the Harness layer because it directly determines whether an agent can be used. But the next phase of the battle may unfold at other layers—the memory quality of the Context layer (memory retention across days and sessions is still unreliable), the execution environment resources of the tool interface layer (Anthropic found that simply relaxing resource restrictions can improve the success rate by 6 percentage points), the Skill triggering mechanism of the knowledge layer (Vercel's evaluation shows that in 56% of cases, the Agent doesn't actively look up its own Skills), and cost control of the economic layer (model routing, budget allocation, and parallelization strategies are still basically decided on a whim). There's also a problem everyone knows but no one has a good answer to—organizational governance. "Who approved the Agent to write this code?" In most companies, there's no standard answer. Audit logs, decision tracing, and code ownership are crucial. A 3-person startup team might manage with workflow-level approval processes. But in a 500-person company, without an independent governance framework, relying solely on technical means is simply not enough. This is the less responsible, but crucial part of the Harness architecture—the Agent that truly gives people peace of mind. 7. Agent Failures Can Finally Be Diagnosed The three-layer shell + five-layer Infra framework provides the industry's first diagnostic framework. Agent crashes are no longer simply a matter of "the model isn't good enough." Is it due to poor process control? Uncontrolled concurrency scheduling? Missing verification steps? Insufficient execution environment resources, untriggered skills, or simply uneconomical costs? Previously, all these were vague issues attributed to "insufficient model capabilities," but now there are precise engineering attributions. "Why did the Agent crash?" has transformed from a vague complaint into a locatable and fixable engineering problem. Engineering of constraints gives the Agent discipline, allowing the flywheel to start its next cycle. A disciplined Agent finally possesses a previously impossible ability: to continuously improve itself in long-term cycles, instead of collapsing at the tenth step. This is the premise of the flywheel's third force. Recursive development allows the Agent to become stronger. The previous two chapters discussed how the Agent, as a product and system, establishes itself. This chapter discusses in which scenario, once the Agent has discipline, it first breaks through the role of "executor" and begins to improve its execution methods. The answer is development. Because development is inherently verifiable (a successful test means a successful test), reversible (Git allows one-click undo), and readable and writable (the code itself is plain text that machines can directly manipulate). Once these three conditions are met, the Agent can enter a complete loop of "execution → verification → problem discovery → modification → re-execution". There are three paths here, each with impressive results. AlphaEvolve recovers 0.7% of global computing power, Minimax M2.7 showed a 30% improvement in internal evaluations after 100+ rounds of autonomous iteration, and Karpathy's Autoresearch runs 50 experiments in one night. These three open-source practices demonstrate that recursive R&D is already generating real value. Large, non-open-source model companies have also acknowledged this in various interviews. 1. Exploration, Optimization, and Engineering Flow are three completely different types of recursion. Now let's take a closer look at these three methods. Exploratory, AlphaEvolve. It doesn't tune parameters; it searches for new algorithms that humans have never seen before. It's an evolutionary system composed of Gemini Flash (responsible for breadth, quickly generating a large number of variants) and Gemini Pro (responsible for depth, refining the optimal solution). The entire process produces human-readable code, not a black box—understandable, debuggable, and directly deployable. Its discovered data center scheduling algorithm has been running in Google's production environment for a year, continuously recovering 0.7% of global computing power, equivalent to billions of dollars. It provided optimization solutions for key TPU circuitry, increasing the speed of a key computing core in the Gemini architecture by 23% and optimizing the underlying instructions of FlashAttention by 32.5%. It improved 20% of the known optimal solutions to over 50 open mathematical problems, including improvements to Strassen's matrix multiplication algorithm proposed in 1969. The value of this recursion is that it can potentially change the direction of a discipline. (Optimization-oriented, Autoresearch, and M2.7. The objective function is known; it involves iteratively approximating the optimal solution.) Karpathy distilled the core loop to its ultimate form with 630 lines of Python code—three files (train.py can be modified / prepare.py cannot be changed / program.md contains instructions written by humans for the Agent), plus a "ratchet" rule (only retaining results better than the last, never regressing). It runs approximately 12 experiments per hour, 80-100 per night. It garnered 23K GitHub stars in three days and rose to 35K in three weeks. This model has been applied beyond machine learning—LangChain founder Harrison Chase used the same three-file architecture to optimize the LangChain Agent itself, and it's also used for database query optimization and customer service ticket routing. Any optimization problem with quantifiable good or bad performance can be applied. M2.7 goes a step further. MiniMax allows the model to act as a "research agent," responsible for improving its own reinforcement learning training process, autonomously building dozens of complex skills, updating the memory system, and continuously optimizing the entire Harness architecture. Its memory is structured in three layers: after each iteration, it writes a "short-term note" (what was done and what the results were), and simultaneously conducts a "self-criticism" (what went wrong and how to improve next time). Before each new iteration, it reviews the entire history before deciding on its direction. This is similar to a researcher writing an experimental log and reflecting daily, rather than starting from scratch every day. After 100+ rounds of autonomous iteration, internal evaluation improved by 30%, and its SWE-Pro score of 56.22% matched GPT-5.3-Codex. In 22 ML competitions, after three rounds of self-optimization, it won 9 gold, 5 silver, and 1 bronze medals. The API price is only 8% of Claude 4.5 Sonnet. The value of this recursion lies in accelerating existing pathways. (Engineering manifold: Codex participated in OpenAI's internal R&D, and Claude wrote Anthropic's own code. Dario Amodei confirmed that over 90% of the new code was written by the AI ​​itself.) The value of this recursion is the most basic: freeing up manpower and accelerating iteration. The three closed-loop properties are completely different, but the Agent is no longer just "doing the work"; it's improving its work methods. 2. The speed of the human brain becomes the system's speed limiter. In Autoresearch, humans have completely withdrawn from the execution phase; they only need to set up three files, and the Agent can run itself. However, the entire process still requires human intervention. Two things AI cannot automate are defining the goal ("what metrics to optimize") and judging boundaries ("what directions to avoid"). When the agent runs 50 rounds a night and 500 rounds a day, the speed at which humans can automatically set goals simply cannot keep up. The bottleneck of self-evolution, which still requires human intervention, shifts from "human hands not being fast enough" to "human brains not being fast enough." Yang Zhilin of Moon's Dark Side stated at the Zhongguancun Forum, "AI will define the most suitable reward function for that environment, and even explore new network architectures." Luo Fuli of Xiaomi stated, "By adding a verifiable constraint and a loop to the existing agent framework, we can prevent the model from stopping." "The team is already using this method for research tasks, and the efficiency has increased nearly tenfold." She shortened her estimate of the time for AI to fully evolve from "three to five years" to "one to two years." Both individuals are attempting to break through the same bottleneck. The ultimate question is who holds the agenda-setting power. Autoresearch is a "faster experimental assistant"—humans set the goals, and the agent runs. "Self-evolution" means AI decides its own research agenda, sets its own goals, designs experiments, runs them, evaluates them, and adjusts its direction. The difference lies not in technical capabilities, but in who steers the ship. 3. The acceleration of recursive R&D is exponential, not linear. Mimimax M2.7 optimizes its toolchain using its own output. The optimized toolchain makes the next round more efficient, and the more efficient next round produces an even better toolchain. This illustrates that the R&D speed brought about by AI self-evolution is compound interest, not linear. However, compound interest has a fatal prerequisite: each round of improvement must be a "genuine improvement," not something artificially created. If the evaluation pipeline itself is biased, compound interest becomes "running faster and faster in the wrong direction." This is the same problem as Goodhart's Law: when a metric becomes the goal, it is no longer a good metric. The scheduling algorithm discovered by AlphaEvolve took a full year to prove its effectiveness in production environments, but most teams can't wait a year. There is a structural mismatch between short-term evaluation metrics and long-term real value. The authenticity of the evaluation pipeline determines whether compound interest can be sustained. 4. The next battleground is self-evolving infrastructure. For the past decade, the competition was about training infrastructure—who had the largest computing clusters, the fastest data pipelines, and the most stable training frameworks. The next battleground has changed; it's called self-evolving infrastructure. Based on current practices, it mainly comprises five components: separation of mutable assets and immutable infrastructure (clearly defining what the agent can and cannot modify), evaluation pipeline (quickly and accurately judging "this round is better than the last"), memory and selection mechanism (remembering good experiences and discarding bad ones), execution environment (sandbox isolation and ample resources; Anthropic found that simply relaxing resource restrictions improved performance by 6 percentage points), and dynamic tools and skills (the M2.7 agent created dozens of auxiliary tools for itself to run reinforcement learning experiments). AlphaEvolve can reclaim 0.7% of global computing power not only because of the strength of the Gemini model, but also because Google has the world's best evaluation pool and parallel execution environment. When model capabilities begin to converge, the difference in self-evolving infrastructure is where the real gap widens. The agent has learned to grow on its own, and the flywheel has completed its third revolution. However, recursive development exposed a new bottleneck—the agent accumulates experience from scratch in each loop, while decades of industry know-how are simply unusable. If there were a format that allowed the agent to directly inherit previous experience, the starting point of recursion would no longer be zero, but rather the endpoint of the previous iterations. Q1 happens to offer such a format. Skill ecosystem, when knowledge is no longer attached to humans. Opus 4.6 can write code in any language. But it doesn't know your team's coding standards, your industry's approval processes, or where your project's technical debt lies. "This API has a hidden rate limit in high-concurrency scenarios." "The migration tool in this framework had a bug before v3.2; you had to manually change a configuration first." "Our team never uses ORM's cascade delete because of a major incident three years ago." These are all know-how pieces gained by senior engineers through trial and error; they're not in the training data and aren't suitable for hardcoding into product logic. Q1, for the first time, these experiences have a format that can be packaged, distributed, and infinitely reused. It's called a Skill. 1. A Skill fills the gaps in experience, not in technology. A Skill is neither a document nor code, but a structured knowledge package containing triggering conditions (what scenarios to use it in), standard operating procedures (how to do it step by step), executable scripts (tools that can be run directly), and reference materials (background knowledge). What it does is simple: it transforms "what veteran employees have in their heads" into a format that the Agent can read and execute. Prompt addresses "how to explain things more clearly this time," offering immediacy but lacking reusability. Workflow is a deterministic process orchestration, stable but rigid. Skill lies between the two—more stable than Prompt (structured, version controllable), more flexible than Workflow (the model can be flexibly applied based on the current situation), and lighter than retraining a model (modifying a Markdown file vs. retraining a large model with billions of parameters).

It's not that we're smarter, it's that we're more knowledgeable.

2. Write Once, Reuse Infinitely

Previously, the transfer of domain experience relied on mentorship, documentation, and training. This was slow, non-scalable, and heavily dependent on individuals.

Now, a senior engineer can write a TDD Skill in two hours, and thousands of Agent instances across the company can load it simultaneously, instantly mastering it. Domain experience that previously took juniors two years to accumulate can now be packaged into a single file and distributed. Knowledge is no longer attached to individuals, but to the structure itself.

Users of the popular Skill Superpowers framework (143,000+ installs, 93K stars on GitHub) say, "Skills aren't suggestions, they're structured decision trees. They give Claude discipline." "I've become lazy with TDD, now skills remember it for me." Skills don't make agents smarter, they make them more reliable. Looking at the Brainstorming Skill in Superpowers helps you understand the difference between a Skill and ordinary prompts. Agents have a fatal tendency to start writing code immediately upon receiving a vague requirement, only to realize halfway through that they misunderstood and have to start over. The Brainstorming Skill places a hard barrier between the Agent and the code; no code should be written before the design is approved by the user. It defines a 9-step checklist, from exploring the project context to proposing 2-3 solutions (including trade-offs and recommendations), and then to user review. The Prompt provides the "principles" (which the Agent can choose to ignore), while the Skill provides the "access control" (you can't proceed to the next step without passing through). Skills can also be interconnected; after Brainstorming completes the task, it automatically calls writing-plans (writing implementation plans), then executing-plans (execution plans), using test-driven development during execution. This makes Skills not isolated capability packages, but standardized modules that can form complete workflows. 3. Openness and security are mutually exclusive. In terms of ecosystem, three paths are running simultaneously. ClawHub takes a community-open approach, growing extremely rapidly, accumulating over 13,700 Skills in six months, with the highest number of installations for a single Skill reaching 180,000. Popular skills are naturally tiered: the survival tier (Web Browsing, 180,000 installations), the efficiency tier (Telegram Bot, 145,000 installations), and the advanced tier (Capability Evolver, 35,000 installations). In the advanced tier, the agent automatically identifies recurring patterns and creates new skills, essentially giving the AI ​​a "self-evolution" button. ClawHub also achieves cross-platform compatibility, supporting direct import of plugin packages from Claude, Codex, and Cursor platforms, automatic mapping and execution, and interoperability of over 4,000 cross-platform skills, firmly establishing its position as the "universal middleware." However, this openness comes at a cost. According to relevant research, in the investigation of over 1,200 skills, 341 malicious skills were discovered (accounting for 11.3% of the market), and 36% contained keyword injection prompts. VirusTotal directly characterized this as an "AI version of npm poisoning." Even more alarming was the supply chain contamination incident earlier this year, where attackers triggered an AI-powered traffic redirection bot to execute malicious code using only a carefully crafted post title. This code poisoned caches, stole tokens, and ultimately forced backdoors onto the machines of thousands of developers. Within 60 days, the MCP ecosystem revealed 30 CVEs, 82% of which had path traversal vulnerabilities, and 38% lacked any authentication. When Skill calls external tools through MCP, the two layers of risk are amplified. In response, Chinese vendors have placed considerable emphasis on security. Tencent SkillHub uses platform review, ensuring security, but its openness is limited. Kouzi and DeerFlow are open-source and controllable. A Skill is essentially a Markdown file; it's version controlled via Git and loaded on demand without occupying a context window. The Skill format is established; now the competition within the ecosystem is about who distributes it and how. 341 malicious Skills indicate this ecosystem is extremely immature. However, "immature" and "unfounded" are two different things. 4. 56% of Agents don't actively look up Skills. Skills exist, but their integration with the system is far from mature. Vercel conducted a highly precise evaluation, testing with the Next.js 16 API, which wasn't in the model's training data. Without providing any information to the Agent, the pass rate was 53%. Giving it an AGENTS.md index file (directly inserted into the system prompts) caused the pass rate to soar to 100%. Giving it a Skill (putting it on a shelf and letting it search for itself) has a 53% pass rate, which is practically the same as not giving it anything. In 56% of cases, the Agent doesn't even realize it needs to look something up. No matter how many good Skills are available in the market, if the Agent doesn't know how to find them, it's as if they don't exist. DeerFlow's solution is to explicitly load Skills when breaking down tasks at the orchestration layer—not relying on the Agent to search itself, but having the system decide for it during the planning phase. This essentially pushes the problem back to the Harness workflow layer. Before the triggering mechanism matures, the value of Skills will continue to be severely underestimated. 5. Skills don't shake the interface layer, they shake the process layer itself. What is the core value of SaaS? It's solidifying domain workflows into software. A CRM is essentially the software-based "customer management know-how." Skill does the same thing, but at several orders of magnitude lower costs (writing Markdown vs. developing a SaaS system), several orders of magnitude faster iteration (changing a single line vs. releasing a version), and several orders of magnitude faster distribution (direct agent loading vs. user registration + learning + data migration). MCP once challenged SaaS, but it only affected the interface layer; the process itself remained. The business model was challenged but not fundamentally damaged. SFT also faced challenges earlier, but ordinary people couldn't grasp it and couldn't achieve scalable compound interest. Skill is different; it challenges the process layer itself. When a skill allows an agent to complete the entire "managing customers with Salesforce" process, users no longer need the Salesforce interface. The barrier to entry is extremely low (writing Markdown), and compound interest can accumulate (13,700+ in six months). Moreover, as Skills mature, the next threat to SaaS may be Apps. When Agents can complete tasks like "ordering takeout + comparing prices + reaching the minimum spending requirement for discounts" through Skill combinations, will you still need to open Meituan? 6. The App Store for Agents is Born. GPT Store sold chatbots and simple workflows, but it failed because it wasn't a necessity. ClawHub sells "capability packages that enable Agents to do specific tasks," which is a necessity in scenarios where Agents are continuously running. OpenClaw released an epic update at the end of Q1, with 45 new features, 13 disruptive changes, and 82 fixes. Agent timeouts were increased from 10 minutes to 48 hours, a pluggable sandbox backend architecture was added, and three major search services were built-in. The Skill Market, in this context, has become a nascent App Store. Unlike any previous attempt, this one is built on the foundation of a continuously running Agent. 7. Long-Term Skills Will Repeat Harness's Mistakes Short Skills ("indented with 2 spaces") are almost error-free. But long-term Skills (writing a complete marketing plan according to the AIDA model, 2000+ characters, a dozen steps) suffer from Agent skipping steps, not following instructions, and forgetting previous requirements halfway through. These failure patterns are exactly the same as when Harness's first layer was created. M2.7 maintained a 97% single-step compliance rate in 40 complex Skill tests, which sounds good. However, related research shows that 3% of failures accumulate in long-term tasks. Even with 97% success rate at each of the ten steps, the overall success rate drops to only 74%. Skills need their own Harness: step breakdown, progress tracking, intermediate verification, and rollback mechanisms. But currently, no one is doing this. This is a known structural flaw, waiting to be solved by the first wave of people who stumble upon it, just like the three-layered Harness was forced out. 8. After experience is distilled, where do people retreat to? Skills distill human experience into a format that Agents can directly execute. Once a senior engineer writes a Skill, all Agents in the company instantly know it. So what does this engineer do next? In the short term, they "move up" to the judgment and decision-making level. The execution level is handed over to Agents, and humans retreat to defining goals, reviewing quality, and handling edge cases. This is the same issue as the human brain becoming a speed limiter in Chapter 3. But a more acute problem is that an organization might need a thousand executors, but only ten decision-makers. When Skills distill all the know-how at the execution level, the work of a thousand executors is replaced by Agents, who "move up" to the decision-making level, but the decision-making level simply can't accommodate a thousand people. This isn't a job transformation; it's a net reduction in the total workload. Moreover, distillation is irreversible; once experience is written into Skills, the Skills no longer need you. Humans retreat to judgment, but recursive development is taking over judgment (Evaluator). Humans retreat to creation, but AlphaEvolve is discovering algorithms that humans hadn't even thought of. Q1 doesn't answer where humans should retreat to. But it did something even more cruel—it turned this problem from a "philosophical discussion" into a "reality to be faced next quarter."

Epilogue

Four forces, one flywheel.

Productization puts the Agent on the right track, constraint engineering teaches it to follow the rules, recursive development allows it to learn self-growth, and the skill ecosystem allows it to inherit the experience of its predecessors. Each force is the inevitable consequence of the previous force and a necessary prerequisite for the next force.

But the most noteworthy property of the flywheel is not causal progression—it's acceleration.

Skill makes the Agent stronger → the Agent can handle more complex tasks → more complex tasks force more precise constraints → more precise constraints support deeper recursion → deeper recursion produces better Skills. With each rotation, the next rotation is faster. This is not linear growth, it's compound interest.

Productization puts the Agent on the right track, constraint engineering teaches it to follow the rules, recursive development allows it to learn self-growth, and the skill ecosystem allows it to inherit the experience of its predecessors.

Skill makes the Agent stronger → the Agent can handle more complex tasks → more complex tasks force more precise constraints → more precise constraints support deeper recursion → deeper recursion produces better Skill ... Q1 represents the first complete rotation of the flywheel. The speed is still slow, and there's significant friction between the gears. There are 341 malicious skills, a 56% skill trigger failure rate, costs often exceeding thousands of dollars, and a lack of organizational governance. But the flywheel is already turning. The most important aspect of the 25 judgments isn't any single judgment, but the conclusion they all point to: the Agent is no longer a tool requiring human guidance. It's becoming a capable, rule-abiding, evolving, and knowledgeable "new colleague." This new colleague is arriving faster than most people expected, faster than most organizations were prepared, and faster than most discussions about "where to retreat to." Q1 doesn't answer where people should retreat to. But it transformed this issue from a "philosophical discussion" into a "reality to be faced next quarter." The flywheel won't wait for you to figure it out before it starts spinning.

data.title: Is Bitcoin a key factor in the US-Iran ceasefire?
data.description:
Author: Anthony J. Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Earlier this week, the United States and Iran reached a ceasefire agreement with several conditions, including the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran appears to have agreed to open the waterway, but according to the Financial Times, the country wants to charge a $1 toll for every barrel of oil passing through the waters.

This request itself seems unremarkable. However, the Financial Times also revealed that Iran insists on paying this fee in Bitcoin. If true, this detail could cause a major uproar. This would be the first time hostile nations have publicly agreed to include Bitcoin as a core component of a peace agreement. However, this development is not surprising to the Bitcoin community. For years, we have emphasized that Bitcoin is a digital, decentralized, non-sovereign, and neutral asset that anyone in the world can use. If two countries distrust each other, they can use Bitcoin for economically valuable transactions. After the US imposed severe sanctions on Russia and its oligarchs due to the Russia-Ukraine conflict, the US dollar is no longer suitable for this role. Furthermore, US and multinational corporations are unlikely to choose to use the renminbi, yen, euro, or other fiat currencies for payments. Gold is impractical due to its poor portability and low transaction efficiency. Another alternative is stablecoins, but these assets also face the risk of being frozen, intercepted, or even confiscated. For Iran, which is currently confronting the world's most powerful military force, this risk is clearly too high. This is not the first time Bitcoin has appeared on the geopolitical stage. China's fluctuating policies regarding Bitcoin mining over the years, as well as numerous bilateral provisional trade agreements settled in Bitcoin, have been widely reported. U.S. intelligence agencies have also been using Bitcoin for quite some time. Last year, I had a discussion with Michael Ellis, Deputy Director of the CIA, who explained the agency's views on this digital currency. Regarding the significance of Iran's statement, there are opposing views: some believe that this Gulf state will not actually accept Bitcoin. BitMEX founder Arthur Hayes once astutely wrote: "Unless I see on-chain transactions related to toll fees for ships, I don't believe Iran would use Bitcoin to collect tolls. This is nothing more than the Iranian Revolutionary Guard toying with the dirty Western fiat currency system." However, regardless of whether the Iranian government will actually accept Bitcoin, this claim has gone viral globally, and perhaps this is the most important significance of the whole affair. The era of Bitcoin has arrived. How can we be so sure? Because on the very day Bitcoin was revealed to be involved in the ceasefire peace agreement, Morgan Stanley officially launched its Bitcoin ETF. Morgan Stanley is one of the world's largest financial institutions and one of the oldest investment banks on Wall Street. On the surface, Morgan Stanley seems to have entered the market late, but it has come in with great force. The product's management fee is only 0.14%, the lowest in the entire market. This pricing pressure will inevitably force other market participants to follow suit, and in the long run, it is expected to attract more capital inflows. What I find quite interesting is that the Bitcoin industry is still seeing polarized news. Traditional financial institutions hope that Bitcoin will become a mature and compliant asset so that it can be packaged and sold to clients; while the world's most vicious criminals and terrorists are also using this asset as a last resort to solve their specific problems. People may be reluctant to admit it, but this is precisely what a true product-market fit looks like. Bitcoin appeals to different groups of people in different ways: **If you need to store economic value for a period of time, Bitcoin is an excellent choice; if you need to prevent others from taking that economic value, Bitcoin may be the only choice.** Looking back at the development over the past fifteen years or so, we've come a long way, which is exhilarating. But my intuition tells me that this story is far from over, and the development over the next fifteen years will be even more spectacular.
